body_txt: Kobolds originate in Germanic folklore, where they were goblin-like spirits believed to live in places where people lived and worked, such as households, shops, mines, or ships. They were a fairly diverse lot, and ranged in personality from helpful House Fey to cruel tricksters. In this meaning, which is still prevalent in German-speaking areas, "kobold" is used as a catch-all term for humanoid fairy beings in European folklore, and in some European languages the term is still used as translation for "goblin", "gnome", "leprechaun", and similar things. In Anglophone media, kobolds remained relatively obscure until they showed up in Dungeons & Dragons , which depicted them as partly reptilian and partly canine beings — eventually shifting to a fully reptilian/dragon-like appearance — and characterized them as clever, inventive, and malicious beings that live underground. From there they spread to Japanese media, primarily through early JRPGs like Wizardry and its successors, which, due to being inspired by earlier editions of D&D , more commonly depicted them as entirely canine creatures. In both cases, due to their origins as mine spirits, kobolds in modern fantasy media are almost always depicted as living underground, either in caves or in complex tunnel systems that they dig themselves. They're often prolific miners, and may be depicted as skilled engineers and crafters. Kobolds are rarely dangerous or powerful creatures. They're generally stuck firmly at the bottom of the Sorting Algorithm of Evil , usually either as minor nuisances or as weaker minions of more powerful beings. They will often rely on cleverness or sheer numbers to face stronger foes. Because of the thematic overlap with the more well-known and humanoid dwarves, it is not uncommon for the two races to be portrayed as natural enemies, similar to humans and ogres/orcs or elves and dark elves. In terms of appearance, they are typically short, goblin-like humanoids that may or may not have varying degrees of resemblance to other mammals, chiefly canids or rodents. However, in works influenced by Dungeons & Dragons , which originally depicted them as doglike humanoids before giving them their current reptilian look, they can resemble miniature Lizard Folk or Draconic Humanoids . For other fantasy races with similar origins and habits, see also Our Dwarves Are All the Same , Our Gnomes Are Weirder , and Our Goblins Are Different .
